nativeRange.habitat: Generally found in shallow coral reef environments, among living coral heads and coral-rubble zones. Typically cryptic by day, sheltering in crevices and beneath ledges, and active at twilight or night. Type locality is Ternate, Indonesia (de Man, 1902).

decorPreferences: live rock with abundant caves and overhangs
mature reef tank with stable parameters
subdued lighting in at least part of the tank

diet.notes: Generally accepts a varied carnivore-leaning diet of small pieces of fish or shrimp flesh, frozen mysis and brine shrimp, and similar meaty foods. Sources describe a wild diet that includes coral polyps, so this species is considered corallivorous in addition to scavenging. Best fed in the evening or after lights-out to match its nocturnal activity pattern.

goodWith: peaceful reef fish that won't harass small shrimp
snails and hermit crabs of similar or larger size
avoidWith: LPS, SPS, soft corals and zoanthids (commonly picked at; not considered reef-safe)
feather duster worms, tubeworms and similar polyp-bearing inverts
small ornamental shrimp and very small inverts (predatory risk)
lionfish, large hawkfish, large wrasses, triggerfish, puffers, eels
Coral Banded Shrimp and Arrow Crabs (territorial conflict)
conspecifics other than a single mated pair

imageCandidates.notes: Cricket Raspet (via GBIF), 2048x1520. Sharp full lateral view of a live Saron neglectus; clearly shows the red/marbled spotted body, toothed rostrum, and closely green-banded pereopods diagnostic of the neglectus complex.

Thomas Irvine (via GBIF), 2048x1462. Striking deep-red nocturnal coloration with cream marbling and gold-banded legs; front-three-quarter view, abdomen partly behind coral so kept as non-recommended color variant.
Jay Paroline (via GBIF), 2048x1536. Green mottled daytime-camouflage morph on branching coral, showing green-banded walking legs and bristly setae; slight top-down angle.

summary: Small nocturnal shrimp from the Indo-West Pacific, recognized by mottled green or brown daytime coloration that shifts to light red after dark. Generally not considered reef-safe, as it has been reported to feed on coral polyps. Closely related to and often confused with the larger Saron marmoratus.
careNotes:
The Saron Shrimp (Common) (Saron neglectus) is a small marine shrimp first described by de Man in 1902 from Ternate, Indonesia. It ranges broadly across the Indo-West Pacific, from the Red Sea and East Africa eastward through Indonesia and the Philippines to Hawaii and French Polynesia, where it inhabits shallow coral reefs from about 1 to 20 metres depth. Adults are generally reported to reach around 4 to 5 cm — somewhat smaller than the closely related Saron marmoratus, which commonly reaches 5 to 8 cm.

Like S. marmoratus, the Common Saron is crepuscular to nocturnal (most active at twilight and after dark) and cryptic by day, resting deep within caves, crevices, and the undercuts of live rock. Its coloration changes markedly between day and night: by day, the body appears mottled green or brown for camouflage among reef rubble; after lights-out, it shifts to a lighter reddish tone. Males develop distinctive tufts of bristly hair-like projections (cirri, or setae) and a pair of stout, enlarged first walking legs (pereopods); a useful distinguishing feature from S. marmoratus is that the pereopods of S. neglectus carry closely spaced green bands rather than the irregular patterning seen in the marmoratus complex. The species-level taxonomy of S. neglectus is still considered unsettled and may be subject to revision.

Care requirements are reported to be effectively the same as for S. marmoratus and consistent with standard reef husbandry: water temperature 24–27°C, pH 8.1–8.4, specific gravity 1.023–1.025, and alkalinity 8–12 dKH. Like all shrimp, S. neglectus is sensitive to copper — any medication or supplement containing copper should be kept out of the tank. Supplementing with calcium, magnesium, iodine, and trace elements is generally advised to support healthy molting. The tank should offer mature live rock with abundant caves, overhangs, and shaded crevices where the shrimp can hide during the day. A minimum tank size of around 100 litres is recommended.

The Common Saron is best kept singly or as a mated pair; multiple individuals are likely to fight. It is not considered reef-safe: wild specimens feed on coral polyps in addition to scavenging, and captive individuals have been reported to pick at LPS and soft corals, zoanthids, feather dusters, and other small invertebrates. It is best suited to fish-only or FOWLR (fish-only-with-live-rock) systems, or a species-appropriate display without living coral. Feed in the evening or after lights-out to align with the shrimp's natural activity pattern.
breedingNotes: No reliably reported captive breeding has been documented for Saron neglectus. By analogy with the closely related Saron marmoratus, larvae are expected to pass through multiple zoea and postlarval stages that require stable, clean larval-rearing conditions outside a typical display tank. Animals available in the trade are wild-caught.

primary.name: ReefLex — Saron neglectus (Eyespot shrimp / Green Marble Shrimp)
primary.notes: Swapped in as primary because the assigned manifest primary (LiveAquaria) and secondary (BlueZoo Aquatics) only stock a generic 'Saron Shrimp' that they tag as Saron marmoratus — neither has a product page distinguishing S. neglectus from S. marmoratus. ReefLex provided: scientific name (de Man, 1902), common names (Eyespot Shrimp, Green Marble Shrimp, Spotted Marbled Shrimp), family Hippolytidae, Indo-West Pacific distribution (East Africa to French Polynesia and Hawaii, including Red Sea, Seychelles, Indonesia, Philippines, Australia, Marshall Islands), depth 1-20 m, size ~4-5 cm, water temperature 25.0-29.3°C, minimum tank ~50 L, diet omnivore/corallivorous, and the note that there are no reports of this species having been successfully kept in captivity.

additional.name: Red Sea Creatures — Green Marble Shrimp (Saron neglectus)
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (~5 cm / 2 in), distribution (Red Sea to Hawaii), depth (to ~12 m), nocturnal activity, mottled green / light red color change, male elongated foreclaws and female curved abdomen, omnivorous diet with coral polyp predation.

additional.name: SeaLifeBase — Saron neglectus (Eyespot shrimp)
additional.notes: Taxonomy (Malacostraca / Decapoda / Hippolytidae), authorship (de Man, 1902, type locality Ternate, Indonesia), Indo-Pacific distribution including Oman, Reunion, New Caledonia and French Polynesia. Confirms benthic, subtidal occurrence.

additional.name: Reef Builders — An Identification Guide to the Saron Shrimps (2024-06-10)
additional.notes: Used for morphological distinction between the marmoratus and neglectus complexes: in the neglectus complex the pereopods carry closely spaced green bands, the maxilliped is pink with white setae, and the males' first pereopods are stout rather than greatly elongated. Source notes the species-complex status of S. neglectus is unsettled pending taxonomic revision.

additional.name: SaltCorner — Saron neglectus (Green Marble Shrimp, Eyespot Shrimp)
additional.notes: Accessed only via search-result snippet (direct WebFetch failed with a TLS certificate error). Used for reef-safety call ('not safe with corals — favorite meal is coral polyps'), nocturnal habit with color change to mottled green / light red, single-or-pair grouping, and feeding guidance (meaty foods such as small pieces of fish or shrimp flesh, once daily).

crustacean.moltingNotes: Sources do not give a typical molt interval. Adequate iodine and trace elements are commonly cited as important for proper molting in Saron shrimp; molts are generally left in the tank to be consumed for calcium reuptake.
crustacean.breedingNotes: No reliably reported captive-breeding success specific to Saron neglectus. By analogy with S. marmoratus, larval stages would require stable, clean larval-rearing conditions outside a typical display tank.
